coredata.dc:title: Unraveling the surface states related Stokes shift dependent electrocatalytic activity of N-doped carbon quantum dots for photovoltaic applications
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Mumtaz
Anjum, Aima Sameen
Riaz, Rabia
Bibi, Ayesha
Sun, Kyung Chul
Jeong, Sung Hoon
coredata.dc:description: Modulating the optical properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), such as blue to red emission, by varying the surface states is widely used for optical devices. However, the role of surface states in the electrocatalytic activity of CQDs remains incompletely elucidated. In this regard, we modulated the surface states by varying the synthesis solvents to obtain CQDs with varying Stokes shift: blue-, green-, and red-emissive CQDs denoted as BDs, GDs, and RDs, respectively. The electrocatalytic activity of these CQDs were investigated. To prevent aggregation-induced quenching of the electrocatalytic activity and enhance the conductivity, CQDs were grown on carbon nanotubes (CNT), which provide multidimensional charge transport channels. The RDs showed the largest Stokes-shift and better electrocatalytic activity than those of the GDs and BDs. The underlying mechanism for the superior electrocatalytic activity of the RDs was related to their effective band tuning, enhanced surface reactivity, and fast charge transfer. The performance of dye-sensitized solar cells employing RDs-modified CNT as counter electrode is comparable to that of a conventional Pt-based counter electrode.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the solvent-assisted synthesis of multicolor quantum dots on CNT (BDs@CNT, GDs@CNT, and RDs@CNT) and their coating on FTO glass for counter electrode application. (b) Blue, green, and red emission of CQDs colloids under a 365 nm lamp. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM images of (a) BDs@CNT, (b) GDs@CNT, and (c) RDs@CNT at low and high resolution (left to right). Scale bar = 10 nm.
SEM images of (a) bare CNT and (b) RDs@CNT. Scale bar = 50 nm. (c) XRD patterns of bare CNT and CQDs@CNT films fabricated on FTO glass. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–visible absorbance and (b) Tauc plot of BDs, GDs, and RDs. (c) Schematic of electron transitions in BDs, GDs, and RDs. PL spectra of (d) BDs, (e) GDs, and (f) RDs. The insets show the CQDs colloid emissions under a 365 nm lamp. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XPS survey of bare CNT and CQDs@CNT. High-resolution spectra of (b) O 1s and (c) N 1s of CQDs@CNT. The arrows heading upward/downward indicate the increasing/decreasing trends of the elemental state, and the solid lines refer to non-varying state. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Electrocatalytic activity of CQDs@CNT determined via cyclic voltammetry. (b) Comparison of the electrocatalytic activity of RDs@CNT and bare CNT, performed at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. (c) Nyquist plots for CQDs@CNT. (d) Comparison of the Nyquist plots of RDs@CNT, Pt, and bare CNT electrodes. (e) Photovoltaic performance of CQDs@CNT. (f) Comparison of the photovoltaic performances of RDs@CNT, Pt, and bare CNT electrodes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Effect of surface charge on the electrocatalytic activity. and (b) Electrocatalytic activities of BDs@CNT and RDs@CNT, respectively. HOMO–LUMO levels of (c) RDs@CNT and (d) BDs@CNT. (e) Mott-Schottky plot of CQDs@CNT. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Raman spectra of CQDs@CNT. (b) Proposed model for the structures of the different types of CQDs, approximated from the analyses of XPS data and optical properties. The RDs@CNT structure favors electrocatalytic activity owing to high conductivity and pyridinic N content in the core. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Summary of the photovoltaic parameters for bare CNT, CQDs@CNT, and Pt electrodes.

head.ce:title: Unraveling the surface states related Stokes shift dependent electrocatalytic activity of N-doped carbon quantum dots for photovoltaic applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modulating the optical properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), such as blue to red emission, by varying the surface states is widely used for optical devices. However, the role of surface states in the electrocatalytic activity of CQDs remains incompletely elucidated. In this regard, we modulated the surface states by varying the synthesis solvents to obtain CQDs with varying Stokes shift: blue-, green-, and red-emissive CQDs denoted as BDs, GDs, and RDs, respectively. The electrocatalytic activity of these CQDs were investigated. To prevent aggregation-induced quenching of the electrocatalytic activity and enhance the conductivity, CQDs were grown on carbon nanotubes (CNT), which provide multidimensional charge transport channels. The RDs showed the largest Stokes-shift and better electrocatalytic activity than those of the GDs and BDs. The underlying mechanism for the superior electrocatalytic activity of the RDs was related to their effective band tuning, enhanced surface reactivity, and fast charge transfer. The performance of dye-sensitized solar cells employing RDs-modified CNT as counter electrode is comparable to that of a conventional Pt-based counter electrode.

ce:section.ce:para: The design of metal-free electrocatalysts with low overpotential and high current densities is a key factor in electrochemical energy–harvesting devices. In this regard, carbon materials compete with metallic catalysts for their earth abundance, high stability, and tunable surface chemistry [1–4]. Carbon nanotubes (CNT) are leading the carbon-based electrocatalysts, providing the advantages of sustainability, stability, and suitable electrocatalytic activity [5,6]. However, the surface functionality and complete dispersion of CNT must be addressed to unlock their complete potential for electrocatalysis. For this purpose, nitrogen doping is a promising way to tune the electrocatalytic activity of carbon materials, thereby modulating the band structure and charge transport properties [7–12]. The size compatibility with carbon atoms and effective electronegativity of nitrogen make this element attractive for tuning the electrocatalytic properties of CNT. Several groups have utilized high-temperature annealing and irradiation in various nitrogen sources, such as ammonia, urea, and cyanamide, to introduce N-dopants into the carbon network [7–12]. Compared to the harsh treatments employed for nitrogen doping, here in this study, we demonstrated the potential of a facile solution-processed growth of multicolored N-doped CQDs for improving the electrocatalytic activity. In addition, the role of CQDs structure in electrocatalytic activity was probed at high resolution.

CQDs are the smallest and most stable species among carbon materials, competing with metallic semiconductors in fluorescent, electrocatalytic, sensing, and theranostic applications [13–15]. Several studies have focused on the optoelectronic properties of CQDs because of their biocompatibility, facile synthesis, stability, and tunable surface chemistry [16,17]. The exact internal composition responsible for the electronic structure of CQDs is highly debated [18–22]. For instance, different approaches have been proposed to modulate the optoelectronic properties (fluorescence and Stokes shift) of CQDs for a broad spectrum of optical devices, such as light-emitting diodes and solar concentrators [23,24]. The crystal size of CQDs is critical for controlling the electronic structure of CQDs. The crystal size determines the width of electron confinement; hence, the bandgap of CQDs varies with crystal size [25–29]. In addition to the optical properties, the size of the CQDs strongly affects the electrocatalytic activity, as recognized by Li et al. [30]. In their study, large CQDs (∼8 nm) showed better electrocatalytic activity than small CQDs (∼3 nm) [30]. This size dependence of electrocatalytic activity was because of the high HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbitals) level in large CQDs, indicating easy oxidation at low overpotentials [30]. Hence, their study proved that the size-dependent electronic structure strongly affects the optical properties and associated electrocatalytic activity of CQDs. Apart from crystal size-dependent variation in the bandgap, tailoring the bandgap by controlling the surface states has emerged as a powerful alternative strategy [18,20,21,25]. Typically, surface states are produced from nonmetallic impurities, commonly N and O, that are attached directly to the hybridized carbon skeleton. The doped atoms can exist on the surface (as a functional group) or in the core of carbon crystallites (doped state). In any of these cases, the doped atoms result in a series of emissive traps and produce intermediate energy states between the HOMO and LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals), reducing the bandgap of CQDs [18,20,21,25]. Adjusting the band structure through the additional N/O energy surface states provides control over the properties of CQDs, with a competitive edge of facile solution processing [20,21,31–33]. For example, the emission wavelength of CQDs can be altered by varying the synthesis solvents because the reaction solvent can change the impurity doping level and associated surface states [18,20,21,25]. The increase in surface states leads to red-shifted band-edge transitions, reflecting a narrow wavelength emission and high Stokes shift [15,31]. The surface states-dependent Stokes shift of CQDs are well understood; however, the influence of the surface states on the electrocatalytic activity of CQDs has not been investigated. Undoubtedly, a deep understanding of the role of surface states is important in designing CQDs-based electrocatalysts.

The performance of CQDs is quenched due to the self-aggregation (without nanosupport) and poor conductivity of CQDs films; hence, conductive nanosupports, such as CNT, are required to utilize the electrocatalytic properties of CQDs. To study the role of surface states, blue- (BDs), green- (GDs), and red-emissive (RDs) CQDs were directly grown on CNT. The differences in Stokes shift and surface states were induced by varying the reaction solvent. BDs, GDs, and RDs were directly grown on CNT using water, ethanol (EtOH), and formamide (FA) as reaction solvents, respectively. The optical and electrocatalytic activity of the CQDs grown on CNT were compared. High pyridinic-N and oxygenated functional groups were observed for the RDs, and these groups provided low charge transfer resistance. Furthermore, the solution-processed, bottom-up synthesis of CQDs enabled the formation of well-defined nitrogen states doped in the carbon network; such N states formation is essential for enhancing the electrocatalytic activity. The performance of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) employing RDs-modified CNT counter electrode was comparable to that of cells with conventional Pt-based counter electrode.

Functional CNT (nitric acid–treated) with an average diameter of ∼10 nm and length >1000 nm were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Other chemicals required for the synthesis of CQDs, including citric acid, ethylenediamine, ethanol, and formamide were obtained from Daejung Chemicals (Korea). The materials required for the fabrication of DSSCs, including TiO2 pastes, N719 dye, and ionomer sealant (60 μm), were acquired from Dyesol (Australia). The chloroplatinic acid for the counter electrode and chemicals for the electrolyte were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Florine doped Tin-oxide (FTO) glass with a surface resistance of 7 Ω/sq was provided by Pilkington.

The growth of CQDs on the surface of CNT was confirmed at high resolution using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100F, JEOL Ltd., Japan). The grayscale measurements of the TEM images were taken to measure the atomic spacing of the CQDs. The modified CNT were further probed using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE–SEM; JSM 6700F, JEOL Ltd., Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. To obtain clear images, the samples were sputtered with Pt for 2 min. The structural crystallinity of the samples was tested using wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) in the grazing incidence (GI) mode. The X-ray source (D/MAX-2500, Rigaku Corporation, Japan) with a characteristic wavelength of 1.58 Å was used in the 2θ range of 10°–80°. The functional groups of CQDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.). The absorbance spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of the CQDs were studied using a UV–visible absorbance spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (SCINCO, Korea), respectively. The charge recombination dynamics were approximated via time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) using FluoTime 300. The elemental composition and chemical groups in the modified carbon heterostructures were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Multilab ESCA 2000 system VG, Thermo Scientific, USA). The XPS instrument was equipped with a mono Al Kα X-ray source with an energy step size of 0.05 V. Cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the carbon structures was performed using a three-electrode system on an electrochemical workstation (BioLogic Science Instruments, USA). The photovoltaic performance of the DSSCs was evaluated using a source measurement system (K10-Lab 20; Mac Science Co., Ltd.). The solar simulator was calibrated using a KIER-calibrated Si solar cell (McScience Inc., South Korea) before performing the test. The structural defects of the composites were analyzed using a Raman spectrophotometer (NWS-3100, JASCO, Japan) provided with a monochromatic (green) excitation laser.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct growth of CQDs on CNT
ce:section.ce:para: For the direct growth of CQDs on CNT, we synthesized CQDs in the presence of CNT. The oxygenated functional groups (–O, –OH, and = O) on the CNT were the active sites for the growth of CQDs. The Stokes shift of the CQDs (BDs, GDs, and RDs) were controlled using different solvents, as depicted in Fig. 1 (a). For the growth of BDs on CNT (BDs@CNT), 0.1 g CNT, 7.5 mL ethylene diamine, and 8.4 g citric acid were dispersed in 100 mL deionized water, and the resulting mixture was sonicated for 30 min using a water bath sonicator (700 W) for dispersion [34]. The given concentration and mix ratio were optimized after preliminary experiments. The finely dispersed solution was then allowed to react at 180 °C for 5 h in a 250 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After hydrothermal treatment, the BDs@CNT were successively washed with water to remove unattached BDs and unreacted chemicals. The BDs@CNT were collected by freeze-drying and then coated on FTO glass by tape-casting followed by thermal reduction, as reported previously [35]. The FTO-coated heterocarbon structures were used as the counter electrode in the DSSC to assess their feasibility for Pt-free DSSC applications. A similar method was followed for the synthesis of GDs and RDs using EtOH and FA as the solvents, respectively [31].

The composites of GDs and RDs with CNT were labeled as GDs@CNT and RDs@CNT, respectively. When discussing any two of the CQDs, only the first letter is used for the first CQD; for instance, when mentioned together, BDs and GDs are labeled B/GDs. To study the effect of surface charge, a composite structure was refluxed with 1 M NaOH and NaBH4 solution at 70 °C for 6 h. By such treatment, excessive Na/O groups can be generated on the surface of the CQDs.

For the fabrication of photoanodes, we used water-, EtOH-, and acetone-washed FTO glass. The washed glass was treated with TiCl4 and fired at 500 °C, followed by tape-casting of the 10 μm main layer of TiO2 (particle size of 15–25 nm). The main layer was baked at 130 °C for 10 min and subsequently coated with a scattering layer of TiO2 (particle size of 500 nm). Both layers were then sintered at 500 °C for 30 min at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The sintered TiO2 was immersed in a 5 mM N719 dye solution for 24 h under dark conditions. The dye-adsorbed TiO2 photoanodes were then coupled with CNT counter electrodes using an iodide-electrolyte shuttle. An ionomer sealant was used as a spacer between both electrodes, and a plasma-treated membrane separator was used to prevent short circuit or peeling off of the carbon materials from the FTO glass, as reported in our previous work [35]. The electrolyte consisted of 0.6 M 1-butyl-3-methyl-imidazolium iodide, 0.05 M iodine, 0.1 M lithium iodide, and 0.2 M 4-tert-butyl pyridine in 3-methoxypropionitrile.

A Pt counter electrode was fabricated by spin-coating 5 mM chloroplatinic acid on a FTO glass, followed by sintering at 450 °C for 30 min. For the case of carbon counter electrodes, 4 mL binder solution (2%) was added to 0.1 g CNT powder, and the resulting mixture was stirred overnight. After uniform dispersion, the resultant carbon paste was tape-casted on the washed FTO glass and dried at 50 °C. Subsequently, the coating was sintered at 300 °C to carbonize the binder to produce a stable CNT film.

The fluorescence in CQDs is commonly controlled either from their size-dependent quantum confinement effect or by modulating the surface states [18,20,21,25]. In particular, tuning the fluorescence by controlling the surface states using different synthesis solvents is an easy approach for synthesizing multicolor-emissive CQDs. During CQDs growth, solvents react with the surface functional groups and vary the N/O concentration in the carbon backbone. The variation in the concentration and states of N/O results in emission colors [18,20,21,25]. To study the electrocatalytic properties of CQDs, a conductive nanosubstrate is necessary; otherwise, CQDs self-aggregate and lose their performance. In addition, the high series resistance (Rs) of CQDs film (without conductive support) limits the electrocatalytic activity. This issue can be effectively avoided through the heterogeneous growth of CQDs on conductive nanosubstrates. For instance, CNT provides charge transport paths and a high surface area to accommodate CQDs without aggregation. To synthesize CQDs on functional CNT, the CQDs growth reaction was conducted in the presence of CNT [Fig. 1(a)]. Such a growth reaction enables the in-situ growth on the CNT's surface. The defect sites or functional groups on the surface of CNT serve as nucleation sites for the heterogeneous growth of CQDs. By using water, EtOH, and FA as the reaction solvent, BDs, GDs, and RDs were heterogeneously grown on the CNT, producing BDs@CNT, GDs@CNT, and RDs@CNT, respectively. The variation in the emissions of the CQDs can be observed through the homogeneously synthesized CQDs colloids under UV light [Fig. 1(b)].

The CNT network provided a high surface area and passivated the CQDs growth, enabling the direct growth of CQDs without the formation of large particles. CQDs and CNT composites were previously synthesized by electrografting, adsorption, coupling-agent-assisted crosslinking, thermal crosslinking, and electrophoretic deposition [36–42]. Such methods require multiple steps, such as synthesis of CQDs, followed by purification, surface activation, and crosslinking by chemical or thermal treatment [36–42]. Above all, the purification and separation processes of CQDs are time and energy consuming; in this work, such processes were avoided by the direct solution-processed growth of CQDs on CNT. We optimized the amounts of the reactants to achieve a controlled growth of CQDs. The one-step facile growth of CQDs enables the facile production and use of these CQDs for electrocatalytic applications.

To confirm the in-situ growth of BDs, GDs, and RDs on the CNTs' surface, we performed TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 . The spatial distribution of the grown CQDs can be observed on the CNT's surface, as clearly shown in the low-resolution TEM images [Fig. S1 (a,b,c)]. All types of CQDs revealed a similar average diameter of 2–12 nm [Fig. S1 (d)]. The size of CQDs is mainly dependant on the reaction time and reactants concentration. Both of these conditions were kept same for all types of CQDs, therefore almost similar size distribution of CQDs was observed. The growth of GDs was higher than other CQDs, which can be attributed to the lower solubility of CQDs in EtOH, as compared to water and FA. However, the growth concentration of BDS and RDs was almost similar, as obvious from Fig. S1. The difference in the crystal fringes of the CQDs and CNT is visible in the high-resolution images in Fig. 2. The fringes of the grown CQDs overlapped with the CNT fringes, whereas the post-crosslinked CQDs (by binder/coupling agent) show the discreet isolated lattice fringes of CQDs [41,42]. Compared to post-crosslinked CQDs, the overlapped interface formed by direct growth provides a strong linkage and fast mutual charge transfer between the CNT and CQDs, as required for efficient electrocatalysis. The lattice fringes of the BDs, GDs, and RDs were observed from the grayscale analysis of the TEM images [Fig. S2]. For all types of CQDs, the overall interplanar spacing calculated from the TEM images was ∼0.20 nm.

The morphology of the carbon heterostructures was analyzed through the FE–SEM images, as shown in Fig. 3 (a and b). Bare CNT revealed a highly aggregated structure [Fig. 3(a)], which was because of limited surface functionality [43]. In contrast, aggregation-free morphology was observed for the CQDs-modified heterostructure, as shown in Fig. 3(b) for RDs@CNT. The grown CQDs are highlighted in the high-resolution image of the RDs@CNT composite structure. Figs. S3(a) and S3(b) show the FE–SEM images of BDs@CNT and GDs@CNT, respectively. The aggregation of CNT was suppressed because of the additional functional groups generated by the grown CQDs. Because of their extremely small size, CQDs can accommodate an exceptionally high content of surface functional groups. Mainly, the CQDs surface contains oxygenated and nitrogenated functional groups, as highlighted in Fig. S3(c). These electronegative functional groups rendered a negative charge, thus providing electrostatic dispersion to the CNT.

The crystal structure of the composite structure was probed using a wide-angle XRD in GI mode [Fig. 3(c)]. The films of the samples were fabricated on FTO glass; hence, low-intensity peaks of the FTO glass were observed, as indicated by the circles in Fig. 3(c). The peaks in the XRD patterns of bare CNT at 26.2° and 43.2° corresponded to the characteristic (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes, respectively. With the growth of CQDs, the intensity of both planes slightly increased [Fig. S3(d)]; this result was attributed to the coexistence of (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes of both CQDs and CNT [44]. In addition, the (0 0 2) plane peak of the CQDs-modified samples shifted to a lower angle by 0.3°. This shift originated from the high lattice spacing of the CQDs edges, which was caused by the mutual repulsion between the electronegative functional groups of the CQDs [44]. All CQDs types exhibited similar XRD peaks (shape and angle); this finding demonstrates that neither crystal structure nor size variations cause the differences in the emissions of the CQDs. Instead, the surface states dominantly cause the differences in the CQDs emissions.

The variation in surface-state-dependent Stokes shift was confirmed by analyzing the optical properties of the CQDs. Fig. 4 (a) shows the normalized UV–visible absorbance spectra of the CQDs; these spectra have three sections: π–π∗ within 250–300 nm wavelength, n–π∗ within 300–400 nm wavelength, and the surface states related to shoulder peaks extending up to 670 nm [45]. The π–π∗ and n–π∗ are caused by electron transitions from the carbon core; therefore, all the CQDs types showed these characteristic peaks. However, shoulder peaks because of surface states were observed in the GDs and RDs only. The role of these CO and C–N states is negligible in BDs, as doping N-doping occurs at the last stage of formation. In contrast, the surface states are more prominent in RDs, as an N-doped carbon skeleton is built at the fluorophore formation stage. This extended visible light absorption is attributed to the surface rich in functional groups, specifically CO and C–N bonds, as highlighted in Fig. 4(a) [21]. For the GDs, only excessive oxidation states (CO) were observed, whereas the RDs showed states related to both oxidation (CO) and nitridation (C–N). These newly formed intermediate states narrow the HOMO–LUMO gap, which facilitates electron transitions at low excitation energies [46,47]. The variation in excitation energy was calculated through a Tauc plot [Fig. 4(b)]. The HOMO–LUMO gap calculated by the x-intercept was 1.94, 2.52, and 3.05 eV for RDs, GDs, and BDs, respectively [Fig. 4(b)]. Based on the UV–visible absorbance and Tauc plots, the possible electron transitions of the CQDs are depicted in Fig. 4(c). The vibrational relaxation and surface states of the RDs were higher than those of the GDs and BDs. Hence, the surface states reduced the bandgap of the RDs, resulting in increased wavelength or high Stokes shift emission. A similar model of CQDs HOMO–LUMO level tuning is reported in earlier reports [18,21,48–51].

The effect of surface functional groups on CQDs emission was theoretically predicted using ab initio calculations [52]. The electronic structure was systematically modified through the strong orbital resonance of surface functional groups and CQDs HOMO-LUMO energy level [52]. Utilizing this effect, the NN-, NH2-, and Diaminonephthalene-functionalized CQDs showed blue-, green-, and red-emissive colloids [52,53]. Similarly, the role of surface oxidation and core nitridation was studied for the CQDs and it was observed that C–O bonds and C–N bonds strongly affect the HOMO and LUMO level, respectively [54]. Similarly, another numerical simulation results revealed that the surface functional groups vary the charge density, and associated photoluminescence is altered by the charge transfer effect of surface states [55]. By increasing the number of –NH2 functional groups from one to five, the bandgap was decreased from 2.5 eV to 2.0 eV [55]. It is important to note that the size of CQDs was kept the same during the calculations. Hence it proves that apart from the size, the surface functionality also can be utilized to control the effective band structure. In other work, it was proved that the red emission is triggered by the graphitic nitrogen-rich centers formed in CQDs [25]. Red CQDs also showed a higher negative surface charge, which was attributed to the higher electronegativity of graphitic nitrogen [25]. Hence, this result was consistent with the previous study, showing the effect of surface charge by introducing different surface states.

The extended visible light absorbance leads to high Stokes shift band-edge transitions, resulting in fluorescence with longer wavelengths. To confirm this phenomenon, we tested the photoluminescence (PL) signal of each CQDs type, as shown in Fig. 4(d)–4(f). The BDs revealed an excitation-independent blue emission centered at 445 nm, indicating the dominance of bandgap fluorescence in BDs [Fig. 4(d)] [56]. A negligible excitation-dependent emission (525 nm) was observed at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm, showing that the role of surface states in the PL of BDs is minor. In contrast, the GDs and RDs showed excitation-dependent Stokes shifts of approximately 115–165 nm [Fig. 4(e)] and 475–600 nm [Fig. 4(f)], respectively. The surface states are formed as defect states or trap sites at an intermediate energy level of the existing HOMO-LUMO level, due to which these newly formed states narrow down the existing energy level. Surface states cause the internal conversion losses, hence a shift in the fluorescence emission is observed, instead of forming a new emission peak [51]. The excitation-dependent emission is because of the strong influence of surface states in the recombination process [57]. The surface states alter the probability of edge transitions when excited with low-energy (visible light) wavelengths; thus, an excitation-dependent red-shift emission was observed. In case of RDs, the red emission is attributed due to C–N states, whose peak absorbance is at 550 nm. Therefore, the RDs colloid under the excitation of 550 nm shows the red emission centered at 650 nm [Fig. S4(a)]. The intensity of this red emission is higher as compared to UV and near-UV excited yellow-orange emission, therefore the RDs colloid color under outdoor light is deep red [(Inset Fig. S4(a)]. Based on the red color under simulated sunlight, RDs are previously reported as red emissive CQDs [29,47].

To further understand the role of surface states, charge recombination dynamics were characterized using TRPL. The fitted values of decay time were monoexponential, showing that recombinations are dominantly radiative [Fig. S4(b)]. Furthermore, the average decay time (τAv.) of BDs, GDs, and RDs was 6.4, 13.8, and 7.8 ns, respectively. The difference in decay time indicates that the difference in surface states has been imparted by changing the solvents [15]. As expected from the UV–visible absorbance result, the oxygenated states of GDs rendered the highest decay time [20]. In contrast, the core conductivity increased by the nitrogenated states, hence RDs show lower τAv.. The PL quantum yield result was consistent with the TRPL results, where the lower τAv. showed a higher quantum yield. BDs, GDs, and RDs showed 85, 26, and 42% quantum yield, respectively [inset table of Fig. S4(b)]. This validates that the surface states not only determine the emission wavelength, but also the concentration of radiative recombinations or quantum yield [15].

For in-depth compositional and structural analyses of B/G/RDs@CNT, we performed XPS, as shown in Fig. 5 . Bare CNT showed no signal for nitrogen; however, after CQDs growth, 5–9% of nitrogen was added to the composite structures [Fig. 5(a)]. The elemental composition of all composite structures was almost similar; however, the difference in the elemental states led to a variation in optical properties. For instance, among the CQDs@CNT, the high-resolution analysis of O 1s [Fig. 5(b)] shows that RDs@CNT have the highest content of CO groups connected to the sp2 hybridized ring (sp2 CO); in contrast, the GDs exhibited the highest content for sp3 C–O bonds, as highlighted in Fig. 5(b). The small arrow of Fig. 5(b) points toward the exceptionally high content of sp3 C–OH in GDs. Because of such oxygenated surface traps, the highest decay time was observed for GDs. In conjunction with oxygenated functional groups, the role of doped nitrogen is critical in determining the optical properties. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s can be resolved in pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen states, as highlighted in Fig. 5(c). Stokes shift was directly correlated with increasing pyridinic-N. The variations in N or O states are because of the reaction solvents, resulting in various emissive chromophore centers.

In particular, the formation of CQDs was completed in three steps: molecular rearrangements, condensation to fluorophores (conjugated colorful molecules), and extended carbonization to CQDs [Fig. S4(c)] [58]. During the carbonization stage, organic solvents (EtOH and FA) are also pyrolyzed, forming a part of fluorophores that serve as emission centers [Fig. S4(c)] [59]. By using EtOH, an increase in sp3 C and sp3 C–O carbon occurs (also observed in the XPS analysis), as represented by the green parts in the structure of GD structure [Fig. S4(c)]. The additional oxygen of GDs extends the excitation wavelength, as the overlap of O 2p orbitals with the conjugated π orbitals results in π + O 2p states; such states reduce the bandgap (excitation energy) [19,54]. Hence, by varying the degree of oxidation in the GDs, a PL emission of ∼550 nm can be achieved [21]. Contrary to EtOH, a variation of both oxidation and nitridation is induced by using FA as the synthesis solvent. Upon pyrolysis of FA, O and N are released, resulting in a remarkable difference in sp2-O and tertiary-N content [Fig. S4(c)] [60]. Compared to other nitrogen states, pyridinic N facilitates conjugation by donating additional electrons [61]. High pyridinic N increases the sp2 hybridization factor in the carbon domains, thus decreasing both the localization of electrons and the associated HOMO-LUMO gap [62]. For the RDs, both N and O states participate in the emission at ∼600 nm. In contrast, during the formation of BDs, N doping occurs at the last (carbonization) stage; hence, the role of N states in the Stokes shift is minimal, as reported in a previous study [34]. The structure depicted in Fig. S4(c) is consistent with the deconvoluted C1s spectrum of CQDs@CNT shown in Fig. S5. Relatively higher content of functional groups was observed for RDs@CNT, which is relatable with higher surface states.

In order to correlate the fluorescent properties with electrocatalytic activity, we performed cyclic voltammetry in an iodide electrolyte using a three-electrode system. Fig. 6 (a) shows the oxidation and reduction peaks at positive and negative potentials, respectively. Both oxidation and reduction peaks at low potentials correspond to I−/I3 −, similar to the electrolyte dynamics of DSSCs. The current density and peak-to-peak potential (E PP) of the left pair of peaks were used to evaluate the electrocatalytic activity [8]. In general, the low E PP and high current density reflect superior electrocatalytic performance. The difference in the Stokes shift imparted significant variations in electrocatalytic activity [Fig. 6(a)]. E PP was inversely correlated with the increase in Stokes shift, as shown in Fig. 6(a). The RD-modified CNT had lower E PP (330 mV) and higher current densities than the BDs@CNT and GDs@CNT. Considering the effect of growth concentration, it can be observed that GDs@CNT has a higher integrated area of the CV curve, which is due to higher surface area provided by densely grown GDs. However, the E PP of GDs is not dependant on the growth concentration. Compared with bare CNT, the optimized RDs@CNT exhibited a significant decrease in E PP and an increase in current density [Fig. 6(b)]. In general, the electrocatalytic activity significantly improved with CQDs growth.

The electrocatalytic activity of RDs@CNT was comparable to that of Pt-coated FTO glass [Fig. S6(a)]. The E PP of RDs@CNT was slightly higher than that of the Pt electrode; for instance, the Pt and RDs@CNT electrodes showed E PP values of 220 and 330 mV, respectively. In contrast, the current density of RDs@CNT was almost threefold higher than that of the Pt electrode, specifically the reduction current density (IRED). The considerably improved current density of RDs@CNT compared to those of Pt and bare CNT was due to fast charge transfer through the grown RDs. An overlapped interface formed by direct growth provides an unobstructed path for the flow of electrons, unlike adsorbed/post-crosslinked CQDs [37]. For comparison, N-doped CNT was also synthesized by treating bare CNT with ethylenediamine in formamide solvent. Citric acid was not added during the reaction, as it serves as the main carbon source for CQDs synthesis. Compared to RDs@CNT, the nitrogen content of N-doped CNT is four folds lower [Fig. S6(b)]. Also, the increase in surface area by N-doping is not significant (compared to CQDs growth), hence the reduction current density of N-doped CNT was two times less than RDs@CNT [Fig. S6(c)]. Another critical parameter of electrocatalytic activity – E PP – was much higher in N-doped CNT. This result confirms that compared to N-doped CNT, the CQDs decorated surfaces show superior performance. The major content of CQDs is also carbon; however, the surface area increase and dispersion of CNT is significantly higher, which improves the electrocatalytic activity significantly.

The loading content of the catalyst was optimized by changing the tape thickness used in tape casting, as shown in Fig. S6(d). The thickness of catalyst film is not significantly affecting the performance; however, relatively better electrocatalytic activity was observed for 3 μm thickness film. At a higher thickness (6 μm), the EPP is decreased due to the higher internal resistance of the thick film. Also, the stability of DSSC is another problem to be considered at higher thicknesses, hence we used 3 μm films.

In addition to the high electrocatalytic activity, the optimized electrode showed reasonable electrocatalytic stability, as shown in Fig. S6(e). Even after 240 cycles, the variation in the electrocatalytic performance was negligible; this result confirms the corrosion resistance and stability of the electrode. We also tested the cyclic stability at slower scan rates in the potential window of (−0.2 – 0.4), as shown in Fig. S7. At higher scan rates, the stability is much higher as compared to slower scan rates. It can be observed that the major decrease in the stability is from the oxidation peak, however, the reduction current density was relatively less affected.

The charge transfer dynamics of the carbon electrodes were investigated via EIS. A symmetrical cell configuration, wherein carbon electrodes face each other, was used for analysis such that the impedances imparted by the photoanodes can be omitted. The x-intercept of the Nyquist plot corresponds to the series resistance (R S), which was similar for all electrodes [Fig. 6(c) and (d)]. R S depends on the conductivity of the substrate, which was FTO glass for all samples. In contrast, notable variations in charge transfer resistance (R CT; the height along the y-axis) were observed [63]. Both R S and R CT were calculated using a Rendells type equivalent circuit model, as shown in Fig. S8. Moreover, R CT was inversely related to Stokes shift; among the CQDs@CNT, the RDs exhibited the lowest R CT [Fig. 6(c)]. A high surface conductivity and reactivity determine the initial barrier for charge transfer at the electrode and electrolyte interface [64]. The R CT of RDs@CNT was remarkably lower than those of BDs@CNT and GDs@CNT. Furthermore, the RDs provided a low charge transfer barrier, which essentially reduced the internal impedance of the solar cell.

The R CT of RDs@CNT was considerably lower than those of bare CNT and Pt electrodes [Fig. 6(d)]. The R CT of RDs@CNT was approximately 13 times lower than that of bare CNT and six times lower than that of the Pt electrode. As shown in the SEM analysis, the CQDs growth enhanced the dispersibility of the CNT; thus, fast electrolyte infiltration ensured that the charge transfer reaction was not diffusion-limited. In contrast, bare CNT had a highly aggregated structure, which restricted the flow of electrolyte; such restricted flow resulted in increased R CT. Additionally, the excellent surface functionality of CQDs provided a strong capillary action for high-speed electrolyte infiltration, thus suppressing the diffusion-dependent impedance [63].

The electrocatalytic activity and R CT of the modified CNT were comparable to those of the Pt electrode; hence, RDs@CNT is a suitable counter electrode for DSSCs. The FTO glass coated with CQDs-modified CNT was coupled with a photoanode, and the photovoltaic performance was tested [Fig. 6(e)]. The values of different parameters for the solar cells employing carbon electrodes are listed in Table 1 . By varying the Stokes shift of CQDs, a major increase in the fill factor (FF) and a minor increase in the open-circuit voltage (V OC) were observed. The variation in current density was negligible because the photoanode structures were similar in all types of solar cells. The high photovoltaic performance of RDs@CNT was consistent with their high electrocatalytic activity and low R CT. The key parameter affecting the performance was FF, which was directly linked to the drastically reduced RCT of the composite electrodes. Moreover, the remarkable electrocatalytic activity of the composite structure provided abundant reduced iodide species near the photoanode. An excess amount of reduced species is required to avoid charge recombination in the excited dye molecules; hence, the electrochemical cycle of DSSCs can be accelerated. The results of cyclic voltammetry, EIS, and photovoltaic performance tests were consistent with the increased Stokes shift and enhanced photovoltaic performance.

The photovoltaic performance of the optimized composite structure RDs@CNT was comparable to that of the commonly used Pt electrode [Fig. 6(f)]. The FF of RDs@CNT was slightly higher than that of the Pt electrode; this result was because of the low impedance provided by the thoroughly dispersed network of RDs@CNT. In contrast, the FF of bare CNT was considerably low, resulting in an overall DSSC efficiency of 5.8% [Fig. 6(f)]. Compared to bare CNT, the photovoltaic performance of the solar cell using RD-modified CNT was enhanced by 62%. The photovoltaic performance of the proposed carbon composite (9.4%) was comparable to that of noble metal–based or metal chalcogenides–based counter electrodes.

Compared to other CNT modification methods, our method provides a facile route for incorporating high N content, resulting in superior performance [Table S1]. Metallic catalysts have high performance [65–69]; however, tons of soil are processed to obtain a few grams of metals. Therefore, the sustainability of such systems is limited. Carbon catalysts are preferable alternatives owing to their earth-abundance, biocompatibility, and chemical stability. To understand the catalytic activity enhancement through CQDs growth, we studied the underlying mechanism of the excellent catalytic activity of CQDs in the next section.

The functional surface of quantum dots is a major determinant of the current density of the electrocatalyst surface. To elucidate the role of surface charge, we compared the electrocatalytic activity of the as-synthesized BDs@CNT after surface modification with NaBH4 and NaOH [Fig. 7 (a)]. NaBH4 treatment increases the number of hydroxyl groups (–OH) on the surface [70]; whereas, NaOH treatment increases the Na concentration [26], as reported previously. NaOH was used because of its electropositive nature and facile attachment on the surface through electrostatic interactions (compared to N-functionalization). We also modified CQDs using HCl treatment and a similar effect was observed for electronegative -Cl groups, as we observed for –OH. Hence, the electronegative surface promotes surface reactivity in CQDs. Both surface treatments resulted in minimal variation in E PP; however, an either increased or decreased current density was observed in a surface modified with electronegative/positive atoms. For the BDs, GDs, and RDs, the surface electronegativity is controlled by the oxygenated functional groups on the surface. The electronegativity difference between C and O atoms causes charge redistribution on the surface, where C and O have partially positive and negative charges, respectively. Compared to a neutral surface, a partially charged surface has a strong interaction with the ionic species from the electrolyte [71,72]. Hence, the surface charge redistribution is directly influenced by the surface reactivity and the number of reduced or oxidized electrolyte species. This effect can be related to the high current density of the RDs because a high content of sp2 C–O was observed for these CQDs. The effect of NaOH and NaBH4 was also studied for bare CNT and the effect of such treatment was not significant in current density [Fig. S9(a)]. This might be due to the lower number of active sites for the variation of surface functionality.

To study the role of the CQDs core structure on the electrocatalytic activity, we compared the electrocatalytic activities before and after thermal reduction [Fig. 7(b)]. Thermal annealing reconstructs the bonds in the carbon skeleton, thereby increasing the degree of graphitization. Unlike surface treatment, the reduction process resulted in a remarkable difference in E PP. The restoration of hybridization by the reduction process controls the electron transfer potential. A high degree of graphitization or high content of sp2 hybridized carbon in the core provides negligible resistance to the flow of electrons, thereby affecting both the current density and E PP [73]. Considering this effect, low E PP and high current densities of the RDs can be expected because of their high conjugation in the core; such conjugation is beneficial for high Stokes shift emission. The high content of pyridinic-N in the RDs provides extra electrons in the core, thereby increasing the conjugation length and lowering the E PP. In contrast, among the CQDs, the BDs exhibited the lowest content of pyridinic N in the core, resulting in the highest E PP and a slow reaction kinetics. A similar trend of higher electrocatalytic activity was observed for the CQDs before reduction, as well [Fig. S9(b)]. Upon reduction, the electrocatalytic activity of all CQDs is increased proportionately [Fig. 6(a)].

One key reason for the improved electrocatalytic performance via CQDs growth is the increased surface area provided by the ultrasmall quantum dots. The surface area calculated using Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) nitrogen adsorption-desorption curve is given in Fig. S10(a). Bare CNT and RDs@CNT showed BET surface area of 86 and 177 m2/g, respectively. Such increased surface area can accommodate a high amount of electrolyte species for redox reactions. Similarly, the superior dispersion of CNT by incorporating CQDs leads to the reduced number of diffusion paths for electrolyte infiltration, thus lowering the internal impedance of the solar cells. In addition, the extent of dispersion strongly affects the electrocatalytic properties of CNT because the sluggish reaction kinetics are omitted via thorough dispersion [63].

The surface reactivity and interface of CQDs@CNT were further probed using water contact angle, as shown in Figs. S10(b–e). The lower water contact angle refers to the higher surface functionality and associated superior electrolyte–electrode interface. In contrast, a higher contact angle refers to low surface energy and hydrophobic character [17,74,75]. The contact angle of bare CNT was around 101°, which is due to limited surface functionality. In contrast, the growth of BDs, GDs, and RDs lowered the contact angle to 30°, 5°, and 12°, respectively. Among CQDs@CNT, the lowest contact angle was observed for the GDs@CNT sample, which is because of their higher growth concentration. In contrast, the BDs– and RDs@CNT although have similar growth concentrations, still RDs showed a lower contact angle, which is due to their higher surface functionality. Overall, the decrease in contact angle was significant by the CQDs growth.

In addition to structural parameters, the electronic structure variation imparted by the CQDs growth can affect catalytic properties. In this regard, the HOMO–LUMO levels of the composite structures were tested using ferrocene as an external reference [Fig. 7(c and d)] [76]. The HOMO–LUMO curve of ferrocene [Fig. S11(a)] was used as a reference for calculating the energy levels of the composite structures according to Equation 1
(1)
E HOMO/LUMO = [(E OX/RED – E 1/2 (ferrocene) + 4.8)] eV

The oxidation and reduction peak potentials provide an approximation of the HOMO and LUMO bands of the composite carbon structures, respectively [77]. The electrochemical bandgaps calculated from the cyclic voltammogram showed that RDs@CNT had a low bandgap, with a shift of both the HOMO and LUMO energy levels. As expected, the HOMO-LUMO level of GDs@CNT was at intermediate energy levels, as compared to B/RDs@CNT [Fig. S11(b)]. Similar to the optical properties, the oxygenated and nitrogenated states of RDs reduced the bandgap and overpotential [78]. The difference in the HOMO–LUMO energy levels was consistent with the decrease in E PP. The accelerated charge transfer via bandgap variation can be explained in view of the d-band theory. For metallic electrocatalysts, a partially filled d-band determines the electrocatalytic properties. However, for carbon materials, a reduced bandgap is an important parameter in the design of efficient electrocatalyst systems [79]. The.

The electronic structures of the composites were further probed through Mott-Schottky plot [Fig. 7(e)]. The positive and negative slopes at positive and negative potentials show that CQDs@CNT had n-type and p-type characters depending on the applied potential. For the DSSC application, electron transfer behavior is important; such behavior can be approximated from the slope at a positive potential. The variation in the positive slope indicates that high-Stokes-shift CQDs caused the shifting of the flat band potential to a lower voltage. The high electron density in the RDs@CNT composite structure facilitates band bending, with an improved n-type character [80]. In contrast, an opposite trend was observed for the negative slope, indicating that the p-type character is higher in the BDs@CNT structure than in RDs@CNT. Therefore, the enhanced electron transport properties of the RDs@CNT assisted in the counter electrode performance.

Raman analysis was performed to determine the variations in the band structure and defects in the composite structure, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). Three bands were observed in the Raman shift: the D band (∼1354 cm−1), G band (∼1587 cm−1), and 2D band (∼2703 cm−1). The D band mainly arises from the TO phonons surrounding the K point of the Brillouin zone; this band is dependent on the defect density. By increasing the Stokes shift, the D band intensity remarkably increased based on the increased I D/G value [Fig. 8(a)]. The frequency shift in the D band was caused by the variation in electronic structure and carrier density [81]. No frequency shift was observed for the BDs@CNT and GDs@CNT; however, a minor shift toward lower frequencies was observed for the RDs@CNT. Such a shift was because of the increased electron density and n-type character. The G band originated from the E2g phonons scattered from the Brillouin zone center, and the band intensity was related to the graphitic/crystalline content of the material. In addition, the softening of the G band (shift to a lower frequency) was observed, similar to what occurs in molecular-charge transfer doping but different from that of electrochemical doping. The G band shift was more pronounced in the RDs than in the other samples. The G-band shift originated from the non-adiabatic elimination of the Kohn anomaly at the G point, and the sharpening of this band was because of the blockage of decay channels of phonons into electron–hole pairs; such blockage was because of the conductivity of the structure [82].

Based on all the experiments to study the mechanism, the excellent electrocatalytic activity of the RDs was attributed to the high C–O states on the surface, highly conjugated core, and low bandgap. In conclusion, a comparative model for the different types of CQDs was proposed in Fig. 8(b). High degrees of surface states and CQDs with high Stokes shifts enhanced the electrocatalytic performance.

Multicolor CQDs were directly grown on CNT via hydrothermal treatment, and the Stokes shift–dependent electrocatalytic activity of these CQDs was studied. The concentration of citric and ethylene diamine was optimized for the controlled synthesis of CQDs, while the Stokes shift was controlled by varying the reaction solvent. The solvent controls the emissive chromophore centers and surface composition in CQDs, imparting a significant difference in the Stokes shift. In general, among the CQDs, the RDs exhibited the highest content of surface states, which reduced the bandgap. The R CT and overpotential of the RDs were lower than those of the BDs and GDs. The relatively high electrocatalytic activity of the RDs was attributed to the high degree of sp2 hybridization, high content of pyridinic–N in the core, and excellent electron transport behavior. Moreover, depending on the Stokes shift of the CQDs, the band structure and charge transport behavior of the CNT were effectively modulated. In summary, all the types of CQDs enhanced the dispersion and surface functionality of the CNT, thus improving the electrolyte infiltration and related charge flow dynamics. Compared to bare CNT, the photovoltaic performance of the RDs@CNT was improved by 62%, which was comparable to that of the Pt electrode.

CQDs modified nanomaterials have great potential in other electrophotocatalytic applications, such as supercapacitors, fuel cells, and water splitting. Similarly, other structural parameters of CQDs, such as hollow CQDs and core–shell structure, can contribute to excellent electrocatalytic performance.
